results.possibleIdentity: Sol Sherr was a prominent electronic engineer and a leading authority on electronic display technology during the late 20th century. He was heavily involved with the Society for Information Display (SID), serving as its Vice President in the mid-1960s and eventually as its President. Sherr was a recipient of the Beatrice Winner Award for Distinguished Service to the Society in 1985 and was a Life Member of the IEEE. His professional career included roles at Sperry Gyroscope in the 1960s and later as the President of Westland Electronics, a consultancy based in Old Chatham, New York. He is also associated with the Hazeltine Corporation and was located in Hartsdale, New York. Sherr is perhaps best known for his extensive academic contributions to the field of display engineering, authoring foundational textbooks such as "Fundamentals of Display System Design" (1970), "Electronic Displays" (1979, revised 1993), and "Applications for Electronic Displays: Technologies and Requirements" (1998). His work on output devices and hardcopy systems, including pen plotters, has been cited in the context of early computer art and technological experimentation, aligning with the activities of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) which sought to bridge the gap between technical engineering and the arts.
